title: The Tudors and Stuarts
overview:
The Tudor and Stuart periods (1485-1689) were one of the most dramatic eras in British history. Religious conflict, powerful monarchs, a flourishing of art and drama, civil war, and eventually the establishment of constitutional monarchy all took place during this time. Henry VIII broke with Rome, Elizabeth I defeated the Spanish Armada, Shakespeare wrote his great plays, and Oliver Cromwell briefly made England a republic before the monarchy was restored.

This lesson covers the Reformation, the six wives of Henry VIII, the reign of Elizabeth I, Shakespeare, the English Civil War, Oliver Cromwell, and the Glorious Revolution of 1688.

study_sections.heading: Henry VIII and the Reformation
study_sections.content:
Henry VIII was most famous for breaking away from the Church of Rome and marrying six times. To divorce his first wife, Catherine of Aragon, Henry needed the approval of the Pope. When the Pope refused, Henry established the Church of England. In this new Church, the king, not the Pope, would have the power to appoint bishops and order how people should worship.

At the same time, the Reformation was happening across Europe - a movement against the authority of the Pope and the Roman Catholic Church. Protestant ideas gradually gained strength in England, Wales and Scotland during the 16th century.

The six wives of Henry VIII were:
• Catherine of Aragon - divorced (Henry needed a son; only daughter Mary survived)
• Anne Boleyn - executed at the Tower of London (their daughter became Elizabeth I)
• Jane Seymour - gave Henry a son (Edward) but died shortly after birth
• Anne of Cleves - divorced (married for political reasons)
• Catherine Howard - executed (accused of taking lovers)
• Catherine Parr - survived Henry and married again but died soon after

During the reign of Henry VIII, Wales became formally united with England by the Act for the Government of Wales. The Welsh sent representatives to the House of Commons and the Welsh legal system was reformed.

study_sections.heading: Edward VI, Mary I and Elizabeth I
study_sections.content:
Henry VIII was succeeded by his son Edward VI, who was strongly Protestant. During his reign, the Book of Common Prayer was written to be used in the Church of England. Edward died at the age of 15 after ruling for just over six years.

His half-sister Mary became queen. Mary was a devout Catholic and persecuted Protestants - for this reason, she became known as 'Bloody Mary'. Mary also died after a short reign.

The next monarch was Mary's half-sister, Elizabeth, the daughter of Henry VIII and Anne Boleyn. Queen Elizabeth I was a Protestant who re-established the Church of England as the official Church. She succeeded in finding a balance between the views of Catholics and the more extreme Protestants, avoiding serious religious conflict within England.

Elizabeth became one of the most popular monarchs in English history, particularly after 1588, when the English defeated the Spanish Armada - a large fleet of ships sent by Spain to conquer England and restore Catholicism.

study_sections.heading: William Shakespeare and the Elizabethan Age
study_sections.content:
The Elizabethan period in England was a time of growing patriotism and cultural richness. English explorers sought new trade routes. Sir Francis Drake was one of the commanders in the defeat of the Spanish Armada. His ship, the Golden Hind, was one of the first to circumnavigate (sail around) the world.

William Shakespeare (1564-1616) was born in Stratford-upon-Avon, England. He was a playwright and actor who wrote many poems and plays. His most famous plays include A Midsummer Night's Dream, Hamlet, Macbeth and Romeo and Juliet. He also dramatised significant historical events. Shakespeare was one of the first to portray ordinary Englishmen and women. He had a great influence on the English language and invented many words still common today.

Famous lines from his works include:
• 'Once more unto the breach' (Henry V)
• 'To be or not to be' (Hamlet)
• 'A rose by any other name' (Romeo and Juliet)
• 'All the world's a stage' (As You Like It)

Many people regard Shakespeare as the greatest playwright of all time. The Globe Theatre in London is a modern copy of the theatres in which his plays were first performed.

In Scotland, Mary Queen of Scots (Mary Stuart) was a Catholic queen who became the centre of a power struggle. She eventually fled to England where Elizabeth I kept her a prisoner for 20 years. Mary was executed, accused of plotting against Elizabeth I. Mary gave her throne to her Protestant son, James VI of Scotland.

study_sections.heading: James I and the Rise of Parliament
study_sections.content:
Elizabeth I never married and had no children. When she died in 1603 her heir was her cousin James VI of Scotland, who became King James I of England, Wales and Ireland (Scotland remained separate).

One achievement of King James' reign was a new translation of the Bible into English, known as the 'King James Version' or the 'Authorised Version'. It continues to be used in many Protestant churches today.

James I and his son Charles I believed in the 'Divine Right of Kings' - the idea that the king was directly appointed by God to rule. Charles I tried to rule without Parliament for 11 years, finding other ways to raise money. Eventually trouble in Scotland meant he had to recall Parliament.

study_sections.heading: The English Civil War and Oliver Cromwell
study_sections.content:
Civil war between the king and Parliament began in 1642. The country split into those who supported the king (the Cavaliers) and those who supported Parliament (the Roundheads).

The king's army was defeated at the Battles of Marston Moor and Naseby. By 1646, Parliament had won the war. Charles I was held prisoner and in 1649 he was executed.

England declared itself a republic, called the Commonwealth. Oliver Cromwell, one of Parliament's generals, eventually emerged as the leader of the new republic. He was given the title of Lord Protector and ruled until his death in 1658. Cromwell remains a controversial figure in Ireland due to the violence he used to establish English authority there.

When Cromwell died, his son Richard became Lord Protector but could not control the army or government. After 11 years as a republic, people wanted stability and began to talk about the need for a king.

study_sections.heading: The Restoration and the Glorious Revolution
study_sections.content:
In May 1660, Parliament invited Charles II to come back from exile in the Netherlands. He was crowned King Charles II of England, Wales, Scotland and Ireland. The Church of England again became the established official Church.

During Charles II's reign, in 1665, there was a major outbreak of plague in London. In 1666, a great fire destroyed much of the city, including many churches and St Paul's Cathedral. London was rebuilt with a new St Paul's, designed by Sir Christopher Wren.

The Habeas Corpus Act became law in 1679. This guaranteed that no one could be held prisoner unlawfully - every prisoner has the right to a court hearing.

Charles II was interested in science. During his reign, the Royal Society was formed to promote 'natural knowledge'. Among its early members were Sir Edmund Halley and Sir Isaac Newton (1643-1727), who showed how gravity applied to the whole universe.

When Charles II died in 1685 his brother James II became king. James was a Catholic who favoured Roman Catholics and did not seek agreement with Parliament. This caused great concern.

In 1688, important Protestants asked William of Orange, the Protestant ruler of the Netherlands and husband of James's daughter Mary, to invade England. James fled to France and William took the throne as William III, ruling jointly with Mary. This event was called the 'Glorious Revolution' because there was no fighting in England and it guaranteed the power of Parliament.

William defeated James II at the Battle of the Boyne in Ireland in 1690, an event still celebrated by some in Northern Ireland today. James's supporters became known as Jacobites.

key_facts: Henry VIII was famous for breaking away from the Church of Rome and marrying six times.
Henry established the Church of England when the Pope refused to approve his divorce.
Henry VIII's six wives: Catherine of Aragon (divorced), Anne Boleyn (executed), Jane Seymour (died), Anne of Cleves (divorced), Catherine Howard (executed), Catherine Parr (survived).
Anne Boleyn was executed at the Tower of London.
Jane Seymour gave Henry his son Edward but died shortly after the birth.
Wales became formally united with England by the Act for the Government of Wales under Henry VIII.
Edward VI was strongly Protestant and the Book of Common Prayer was written during his reign.
Mary I was a devout Catholic who persecuted Protestants and was known as 'Bloody Mary'.
Elizabeth I defeated the Spanish Armada in 1588.
William Shakespeare (1564-1616) was born in Stratford-upon-Avon.
Shakespeare's famous plays include A Midsummer Night's Dream, Hamlet, Macbeth and Romeo and Juliet.
The Globe Theatre in London is a modern copy of Shakespeare's theatre.
Sir Francis Drake's ship, the Golden Hind, circumnavigated the world.
Mary Queen of Scots was executed, accused of plotting against Elizabeth I.
Elizabeth I never married and was succeeded by James VI of Scotland (James I of England).
The King James Bible was an important achievement of James I's reign.
Charles I believed in the Divine Right of Kings and tried to rule without Parliament.
The English Civil War began in 1642.
Supporters of the king were called Cavaliers; Parliament supporters were called Roundheads.
Charles I was executed in 1649.
England became a republic called the Commonwealth.
Oliver Cromwell was Lord Protector until his death in 1658.
Charles II was restored as king in May 1660.
In 1665, there was a major plague outbreak in London.
In 1666, the Great Fire of London destroyed much of the city including St Paul's Cathedral.
Sir Christopher Wren designed the new St Paul's Cathedral.
The Habeas Corpus Act of 1679 guaranteed no one could be held prisoner unlawfully.
The Royal Society was formed during Charles II's reign.
Isaac Newton showed how gravity applied to the whole universe.
The Glorious Revolution was in 1688.
William of Orange became William III after James II fled to France.
The Battle of the Boyne was in 1690.
James II's supporters were known as Jacobites.
memory_hook: Remember Henry's wives with 'Divorced, Beheaded, Died, Divorced, Beheaded, Survived'. For the period overall: 'HENRY REFORMED, ELIZABETH TRIUMPHED, CHARLES LOST HIS HEAD, WILLIAM GLORIOUS': Henry VIII broke with Rome, Elizabeth I beat Armada 1588, Civil War 1642-49, Charles I executed, Glorious Revolution 1688.

questions.prompt: Why did Henry VIII establish the Church of England?
options: The Pope refused to approve his divorce from Catherine of Aragon
He wanted to become head of the Church
questions.explanation: Henry needed the Pope's approval to divorce Catherine of Aragon. When the Pope refused, Henry established the Church of England.

questions.prompt: What happened to Anne Boleyn?
options: She was executed at the Tower of London
questions.explanation: Anne Boleyn was executed at the Tower of London.

questions.prompt: Which wife gave Henry VIII a son?
questions.explanation: Jane Seymour gave Henry the son he wanted (Edward) but died shortly after the birth.

questions.prompt: Which of Henry's wives survived him?
questions.explanation: Catherine Parr was a widow who married Henry late in his life. She survived him.

questions.prompt: What was Mary I known as?
questions.explanation: Mary I was a devout Catholic who persecuted Protestants and became known as 'Bloody Mary'.

questions.prompt: When did England defeat the Spanish Armada?
questions.explanation: In 1588, the English defeated the Spanish Armada under Elizabeth I.

questions.prompt: Where was Shakespeare born?
questions.explanation: William Shakespeare was born in Stratford-upon-Avon, England.

questions.prompt: When did Shakespeare live?
questions.explanation: Shakespeare lived from 1564 to 1616.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a Shakespeare play?
questions.explanation: A Midsummer Night's Dream is one of Shakespeare's most famous plays.

questions.prompt: What is the Globe Theatre?
options: A modern copy of the theatre where Shakespeare's plays were first performed
questions.explanation: The Globe Theatre in London is a modern copy of the theatres in which Shakespeare's plays were first performed.

questions.prompt: What happened to Mary Queen of Scots?
options: She was executed, accused of plotting against Elizabeth I
She was imprisoned by James I
questions.explanation: Mary was kept prisoner for 20 years then executed, accused of plotting against Elizabeth I.

questions.prompt: Who succeeded Elizabeth I?
options: James VI of Scotland (James I of England)
questions.explanation: When Elizabeth died in 1603, her heir was her cousin James VI of Scotland, who became James I of England.

questions.prompt: What was the King James Bible?
options: A new translation of the Bible into English
A book of laws made by James I
A history of the Church of England
questions.explanation: One achievement of King James's reign was a new translation of the Bible into English, known as the King James or Authorised Version.

questions.prompt: What did Charles I believe in?
questions.explanation: Charles I believed in the Divine Right of Kings - that the king was directly appointed by God and did not need Parliament's approval.

questions.prompt: When did the English Civil War begin?
questions.explanation: Civil war between the king and Parliament began in 1642.

questions.prompt: What were the king's supporters called?
questions.explanation: Supporters of the king were called Cavaliers; Parliament's supporters were called Roundheads.

questions.prompt: What happened to Charles I in 1649?
questions.explanation: Charles I was executed in 1649 after Parliament won the Civil War.

questions.prompt: What was the English Republic called?
questions.explanation: England declared itself a republic called the Commonwealth.

questions.prompt: What title was Oliver Cromwell given?
questions.explanation: Oliver Cromwell was given the title of Lord Protector and ruled until his death in 1658.

questions.prompt: When was Charles II restored as king?
questions.explanation: In May 1660, Parliament invited Charles II to come back from exile.

questions.prompt: What happened in London in 1666?
options: The Great Fire destroyed much of the city
questions.explanation: In 1666, a great fire destroyed much of the city including many churches and St Paul's Cathedral.

questions.prompt: Who designed the new St Paul's Cathedral?
questions.explanation: London was rebuilt with a new St Paul's, which was designed by Sir Christopher Wren.

questions.prompt: What did the Habeas Corpus Act 1679 guarantee?
options: No one could be held prisoner unlawfully
questions.explanation: The Habeas Corpus Act guaranteed that no one could be held prisoner unlawfully - every prisoner has a right to a court hearing.

questions.prompt: What was the Royal Society formed to promote?
questions.explanation: The Royal Society was formed during Charles II's reign to promote 'natural knowledge'.

questions.prompt: What was the Glorious Revolution?
options: When William of Orange took the throne from James II in 1688
When Parliament defeated the king in the Civil War
When the Bill of Rights was signed
questions.explanation: In 1688, William of Orange took the English throne from James II without fighting in England.

questions.prompt: When was the Battle of the Boyne?
questions.explanation: William defeated James II at the Battle of the Boyne in Ireland in 1690.

questions.prompt: What were James II's supporters called?
questions.explanation: James's supporters became known as Jacobites.

supportive_messages.high_score: Brilliant! You know the Tudors and Stuarts inside out! 👑
supportive_messages.medium_score: Good work! Focus on Henry's wives and the Civil War timeline! 📚
supportive_messages.low_score: Keep studying - the Tudors and Stuarts are full of dramatic events! 💪
